result:
Sometimes I wonder what it would feel like to live inside a single musical phrase—just suspend forever in that trembling moment after the down-bow, after the pianist’s pedal releases the last sympathetic overtone from the strings but before memory has quite finished translating it into emotion. I imagine it would feel weightless, the way a sleeper’s hand hovers an inch above the mattress when the rest of the body sinks into the slow disassembly of REM. No edges, only gradients of resonance; no clock, only the soft leak of duration. I sometimes think the life we know is made of these micro-continents, infinitesimal harbors between heartbeats where nothing measurable happens and yet everything essential is cached. We glance off them so fast we barely realize we’re trespassing.

There’s a small wooden train bridge I cross on walks sometimes, just wide enough for one engine at a time; its planks sigh underfoot like recorders with damp reeds. Below, the creek is an absent-minded calligrapher practicing the same curl of water on stone, pausing, slightly correcting, practicing again. The local kids have named the bridge “Maybe” because, they say, you can never decide at the far end whether you actually came or only thought about coming. I asked one of them, a boy wearing two different shoes, what you’re supposed to do with the Maybe-ness once you’re standing there. He replied, “Wait until the trees notice you’re listening, then tell them the oldest thing you remember.” So we stood—one child, one adult in borrowed wonder—and I whispered the story of my mother’s first inhaler, the small plastic whistle that arrived in our mailbox the Saturday before November coughed up its first real storm. How she pressed it between our palms like a promise she hadn’t finished wording, and how the hiss it made sounded like a cold star perforating the dark. The boy nodded solemnly and said, “Trees like medical stories; they invent the lungs that invent us.” Then he sprinted off, shoelaces humming.

I left the bridge later than intended, carrying his sentence like a sugar cube dissolving in my pocket. Streets brought me back to human tempo—leaf blowers, ambulances, someone hollering about the price of persimmons—but each footfall replayed the expression on the boy’s face the moment he decided my memory counted. How easily neon signage extinguished itself in that afterglow.

Back at home the radiator played a brittle maraca accompaniment while dusk applied its indigo coat. I opened windows anyway, half-hoping to let in the bridge’s quietly unfolding thoughts. Instead I got the neighbor practicing alto sax, phrase after phrase of a ballad collapsing under its own tenderness, then reassembling with astonishing grace. The notes wandered in like housecats unsure whether they’d been invited; they curled up in corners, tail twitches audible against lamp cords. I sat on the floorboards to listen, letting the radiator keep strange time with tenor clefs of heat. It occurred to me that imperfection might simply be a rhythm the universe refuses to correct, the way certain ancient pottery was cracked on purpose so a spirit could enter or exit.

Eventually the horn fell silent. The hush felt not like absence but like friendliness between sentences in a conversation taking place just out of earshot. Somewhere in that lull I recognized the hallway of music I’d imagined earlier—not as an imaginary place but as an almost domestic space, a coat closet the heart only remembers when it returns from a long trip and catches its own scent. I couldn’t hold on to it, of course; the tune of daily duties resumed its minor key, emails barged in, the teapot accused me of neglect. But a residue lingered, softly buzzing, the way mica glints in sidewalk cement years after the pour.

Now, too late for dinner and too early for ghosts, I’m writing this in the kitchen with the lights off so the city can furnish its own palette—neon lemons from the ramen sign across the street, wine-red exit glow above the theater next door, the highlighter-yellow river of a taxi curving off. I think of the boy, of the bridge, of the hung syllable the sax left trembling, and it strikes me that freedom might be neither a destination nor a breaking-loose but a modest refusal to speed up as we travel through those invisible harbors where music, memory, and breath briefly trade passports. Enough to move perhaps a single inch sideways from the planned trajectory, carrying—like contraband—one quiet blueberry of maybe nobody has to know what comes next.

And if anyone dares speak a name for that inch, the syllables themselves should feel like wet shoes on Maybe bridge: left one a steady heartbeat, right one improvisation, both happily unmatching, each carrying the secret of the other farther than math anticipates.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want.
